retrieved_docs.title: Physical and Chemical Lesions of the Oral Mucosa -- Continuing Education Activity
retrieved_docs.content: Physical and chemical lesions of the oral cavity are frequently identified during routine examinations. Common causes include continuous exposure to trauma, chemical agents, traumatic occlusion, sharp occlusal anatomy, acidic and alkaline substances, medications, and food or beverages at varying temperatures. Depending on the nature and extent of the insult, the lesions may present with symptoms such as pain, ulceration, erythema, or swelling.

retrieved_docs.title: Tooth Fracture -- Deterrence and Patient Education
retrieved_docs.content: Maintaining good dental hygiene and going to regular dental visits help prevent complications by reducing the risk of infection, promoting periodontal health, and ensuring early detection of potential issues. After treatment, patients should be advised to follow proper oral care practices, avoid excessive biting forces on the affected tooth, and adhere to scheduled follow-up visits. Promptly reporting any signs of pain, discoloration, or mobility is essential for early intervention and long-term success.

retrieved_docs.title: Physical and Chemical Lesions of the Oral Mucosa -- Etiology
retrieved_docs.content: Traumatic fibromas are hyperplastic, tumor-like lesions that develop in response to local trauma or irritation. These growths belong to a group of lesions known as reactive hyperplastic lesions of the oral cavity. Traumatic fibromas typically occur in the buccal mucosa and tongue, which are repetitively subjected to trauma, specifically occlusal trauma. [2] Reactive hyperplastic lesions of the oral cavity may also be found on the gingiva. [3]

retrieved_docs.title: Physical and Chemical Lesions of the Oral Mucosa -- Pathophysiology
retrieved_docs.content: Traumatic fibromas develop due to local irritation or injury, leading to progressive fibrosis as the tissue attempts to protect itself. An inflammatory infiltrate, localized ulceration, or edema may accompany this process.

retrieved_docs.title: Dental Emergencies -- History and Physical -- Tooth Luxations
retrieved_docs.content: As it takes a significant amount of force to cause an extrusive, intrusive, or lateral tooth luxation, a thorough examination of the oral cavity should be performed to evaluate for other traumatic injuries, including lacerations, gingival contusions, and mandibular or midface fractures. Radiographs may be useful for assessing the exact positioning of the tooth and also aid in identifying the extent of the injury.

retrieved_docs.title: Physical and Chemical Lesions of the Oral Mucosa -- Introduction
retrieved_docs.content: Physical and chemical lesions of the oral cavity are frequently identified during routine examinations, as the oral cavity is continuously subjected to trauma and chemical agents. Oral cavity lesions can result from various etiologies, including traumatic occlusion, sharp occlusal anatomy, acidic and alkaline substances or medications, thermal or chemical exposures, and improper oral habits, such as using poorly fitted dentures and displaying parafunctional activities. [1]

retrieved_docs.title: Physical and Chemical Lesions of the Oral Mucosa -- Treatment / Management
retrieved_docs.content: TUGSE may be treated with antibiotics to reduce the microbial load on the lesion surface. [25] The lesion should be closely monitored, and any etiologic traumatic agents, such as malocclusion, sharp cusps on teeth, and local irritants, should be addressed. If the lesion does not resolve within 2 weeks, a biopsy is necessary for a definitive diagnosis. In infants, neonatal teeth may be smoothed or reshaped under local anesthesia if indicated.

retrieved_docs.title: Overview of Periodontal Surgical Procedures -- Complications -- Pocket Reduction Surgeries
retrieved_docs.content: Complications specific to periodontal surgery include the possibility that the surgery may not achieve the desired outcome and the unique risks associated with each type of periodontal surgery. Pocket depth reduction surgeries typically result in some degree of gingival recession, leading to a longer appearance of the teeth and exposed root surfaces.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 1 -- Level 1 -- Community 225
retrieved_docs.content: Extraction of single tooth is a direct procedure performed on zuby (teeth), indicating that the teeth are the specific anatomical site for this dental intervention. Disorders of teeth and jaw are described as having zuby as their parent category, meaning that conditions affecting the teeth and jaw are organized under the broader classification of zuby. Consequently, zuby functions both as the procedural target for single‑tooth extraction and as the foundational element within the hierarchy of dental and jaw disorders, underscoring its central importance in dental treatment and pathology.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 9 -- Level 2 -- Community 591
retrieved_docs.content: Accidental physical contact (event) is described as occurring due to verlies; tanden door ongeval, indicating that loss of teeth caused by an accident can precipitate such contact. The phrase “Due to” is used to associate both verlies; tanden door ongeval and crushing injury or internal injury, showing that these conditions are commonly cited as causal factors. Additionally, crushing injury or internal injury is linked with إصابة, the Arabic term for injury, underscoring that these mechanisms represent forms of bodily injury. Together, these relationships illustrate how dental loss from accidents, crushing forces, and internal trauma are interrelated causes of accidental physical contact and overall injury.
